an1.41:1.1: "*Bhikkhus*, just as a rice-stalk or a barley-stalk that is wrongly directed, when pressed by hand or foot, will pierce the hand or foot and draw blood — that is not possible.
an1.41:1.3: Because the stalk is wrongly directed, *bhikkhus*.
an1.41:1.4: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, that a *bhikkhu* with a wrongly directed mind [*citta*] will pierce through blindness [*avijjā*], give rise to knowledge, and realize *nibbāna* — that is not possible.
an1.41:1.6: Because the mind [*citta*] is wrongly directed, *bhikkhus*."
an1.42:1.1: "*Bhikkhus*, just as a rice-stalk or a barley-stalk that is rightly directed, when pressed by hand or foot, will pierce the hand or foot and draw blood — that is possible.
an1.42:1.3: Because the stalk is rightly directed, *bhikkhus*.
an1.42:1.4: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, that a *bhikkhu* with a rightly directed mind [*citta*] will pierce through blindness [*avijjā*], give rise to knowledge, and realize *nibbāna* — that is possible.
an1.42:1.6: Because the mind [*citta*] is rightly directed, *bhikkhus*."
an1.43:1.1: "Here, *bhikkhus*, I know a certain person whose mind [*citta*] is corrupted, by encompassing their mind [*citta*] with my own mind [*citta*]:
an1.43:1.2: 'If this person were to die at this moment, they would be deposited accordingly — in hell.'
an1.43:1.5: It is on account of a corrupted mind [*citta*], *bhikkhus*, that some beings here, at the breaking up of the body after death, are reborn in a state of loss, an unhappy destination, a lower realm, in hell."
an1.44:1.1: "Here, *bhikkhus*, I know a certain person whose mind [*citta*] is serene, by encompassing their mind [*citta*] with my own mind [*citta*]:
an1.44:1.2: 'If this person were to die at this moment, they would be deposited accordingly — in heaven.'
an1.44:1.5: It is on account of a serene mind [*citta*], *bhikkhus*, that some beings here, at the breaking up of the body after death, are reborn in a happy destination, in the heavenly world."
an1.45:1.1: "*Bhikkhus*, just as if there were a lake of water that was turbid, stirred up, and muddy — a person standing on the bank with good eyesight would not be able to see oysters and shells, gravel and pebbles, or schools of fish, moving about or standing still.
an1.45:1.4: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, that a *bhikkhu* with a turbid mind [*citta*] will know their own good, or know another's good, or know the good of both, or realize a distinction in knowledge and vision surpassing the human — that is not possible.
an1.46:1.1: "*Bhikkhus*, just as if there were a lake of water that was clear, limpid, and undisturbed — a person standing on the bank with good eyesight would be able to see oysters and shells, gravel and pebbles, or schools of fish, moving about or standing still.
an1.46:1.4: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, that a *bhikkhu* with an undisturbed mind [*citta*] will know their own good, or know another's good, or know the good of both, or realize a distinction in knowledge and vision surpassing the human — that is possible.
an1.47:1.1: "*Bhikkhus*, among all kinds of trees, the tendril is said to be foremost in pliability and workability.
an1.47:1.2: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, I see no other single thing that, when developed and cultivated, becomes so pliant and workable as the mind [*citta*].
an1.47:1.3: The mind [*citta*], *bhikkhus*, when developed and cultivated, becomes pliant and workable."
an1.48:1.1: "*Bhikkhus*, I see no other single thing that changes so quickly as the mind [*citta*].
an1.48:1.2: It is not easy, *bhikkhus*, to find a simile for how quickly the mind [*citta*] changes."
an1.49:1.2: And yet it is defiled by adventitious defilements."
an1.50:1.2: And it is freed from adventitious defilements."
an1.50:1.4: The Chapter on Wrongly Directed, the fifth.
